transparent.title: Transparent, Translucent and Opaque Objects
transparent.content:
Have you ever wondered why you can see through glass but not through a wall? It all depends on how much light passes through a material!

When light falls on an object, three things can happen. Some materials allow almost all light to pass through them — we call these **transparent** materials. Clear glass, water, and air are great examples. You can see objects on the other side very clearly.

Some materials allow only some light to pass through, and they scatter it — these are called **translucent** materials. Frosted glass, oiled paper, and thin cloth are translucent. You know there's something on the other side, but it looks blurry.

Materials that don't let any light pass through are called **opaque**. Wood, metal, and thick cardboard are opaque. You cannot see through them at all.

In our everyday life, windows in classrooms are transparent so students can see outside. Bathroom windows are often translucent for privacy. Walls are opaque to keep rooms separate.
transparent.tryAtHome: Hold a glass of water, a sheet of paper, and a thick book in front of a torch one by one. See how much light passes through each. Which one is transparent? Which is opaque?

shadows.title: How Shadows Are Formed
shadows.content:
On a sunny day in your school playground, have you noticed a dark shape following you everywhere? That's your shadow — and it forms because of something really cool about light!

Light travels in straight lines. When an **opaque object** comes in the path of light, it blocks the light rays. The region behind the object where light cannot reach becomes dark. This dark region is called a **shadow**.

For a shadow to form, you need three things: a **source of light** (like the sun or a torch), an **opaque object** (like your body or a ball), and a **screen** where the shadow falls (like the ground or a wall).

Notice that transparent objects like glass don't form clear shadows — that's because light passes right through them! Only opaque objects block light well enough to cast proper shadows.

The shadow always forms on the **opposite side** of the object from the light source.
shadows.tryAtHome: On a sunny day, go outside with a friend. Notice where your shadows fall. Try facing different directions. Does your shadow always appear on the same side as the sun, or the opposite side?

shadow_properties.title: Properties of Shadows
shadow_properties.content:
Shadows are fascinating — they change shape, size, and even position! Let's explore what affects a shadow.

**Colour:** Shadows are always black or very dark, regardless of the colour of the object. This is because a shadow is simply the **absence of light** — it's a region where light doesn't reach.

**Shape:** The shape of a shadow depends on the shape of the object and the angle of light. A round ball always casts a round shadow, but a pencil can cast a long shadow or a circular one depending on how you hold it!

**Size:** The size of a shadow depends on how close the object is to the light source. Move the object **closer to the light** — the shadow becomes **bigger**. Move it **further away** — the shadow becomes **smaller**. That's why your shadow is longest in the morning and evening (sun near horizon) and shortest at noon (sun overhead).

In the morning when Aman walks to school, his shadow stretches out long in front of him. By afternoon, it shrinks to a tiny patch beneath his feet!
shadow_properties.tryAtHome: At home tonight, use a torch and a toy. Hold the toy close to the torch and look at its shadow. Now slowly move the toy away from the torch. What happens to the shadow size?

pinhole.title: Pinhole Camera
pinhole.content:
Did you know you can make a camera without any lens or electronics? The pinhole camera is one of the oldest inventions in the world, and it works purely because light travels in straight lines!

A pinhole camera is a **dark box** with a tiny hole on one side and a screen (or white paper) on the opposite side. When you point it at a bright object, light from the top of the object passes through the hole and hits the **bottom** of the screen. Light from the bottom of the object hits the **top** of the screen.

This means the image formed is **inverted** (upside down) and also **laterally reversed** (left and right swapped). The image is real — meaning it actually forms on the screen!

Why must the hole be tiny? A large hole lets too many light rays in from different directions, creating an overlapping blurry mess. A small hole lets only specific rays through, creating a sharp image.

Photographers still use this principle today, and your eye's pupil works in a similar way!
pinhole.tryAtHome: Take a shoebox and make a tiny pinhole on one end. Place tracing paper on the other end. Point it at a window on a bright day. Can you see an inverted image of the outside?

reflection.title: Reflection of Light
reflection.content:
When you look into a mirror, you see yourself — but how? The answer is **reflection** — when light bounces off a surface!

Light travels until it hits a surface. If the surface is very **smooth and shiny** (like a mirror), light bounces back in a regular pattern. This is called **regular reflection** and it forms a clear image.

If the surface is **rough and uneven** (like a wall), light scatters in all directions. This is called **irregular reflection** or diffuse reflection. You won't see your image in a rough wall!

Mirrors are made of glass with a silver coating behind it. The silver is extremely smooth, so it reflects light perfectly.

The most amazing example of reflection is the **Moon**! The moon has no light of its own. It reflects sunlight towards Earth. That's why we can see the moon at night — we're actually seeing reflected sunlight!

Reflection is used in rear-view mirrors in cars, in periscopes used by submarines, and even in solar cookers that focus reflected sunlight to cook food.
reflection.tryAtHome: Stand in front of a mirror and raise your right hand. Which hand does your image raise? Now write your name on paper and hold it in front of the mirror. What do you notice?

day_night.title: Day and Night
day_night.content:
Why do we have day and night? Why does the sun seem to move across the sky? The answer is Earth's rotation!

The **Earth is shaped like a ball** (a sphere). The **Sun** is a giant star that gives out light. At any moment, only the **half of Earth facing the Sun** receives sunlight — this half experiences **day**. The other half, facing away from the sun, is in darkness — this half experiences **night**.

The Earth **rotates on its own axis** — an imaginary line through the North and South Poles. It completes one full rotation in about **24 hours** — this is what gives us one day!

As Earth rotates, different parts of its surface face the Sun. When Delhi faces the Sun, it is daytime there. Exactly 12 hours later, Delhi has rotated to face away from the Sun, and it becomes night.

The Sun doesn't actually move — it's the Earth spinning that makes the Sun appear to rise in the East and set in the West every day.

This is also why when it is day in India, it is night in the USA — they are on opposite sides of the Earth!
day_night.tryAtHome: Take a torch and a ball (like a cricket ball). In a dark room, shine the torch on the ball. Notice which half is lit up and which is dark. Slowly rotate the ball — can you see how day and night change?
